speaker: interviewer
text: --For Traub's interview. You were going to tell us what happened at the Weberei.

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked at the Weberei until about-- again, I cannot give you the exact month. But it was fall. It was very cruel work, because we were given-- well, let me say what we were supposed to do in the Weberei. We were supposed to make long, rope-type things out of old clothes, tear it up and then sort of put it together, knot it together, and then make rolls out of it. Until this day, I don't know what the purpose of it was, except I think they were used for some sort of fuses. I'm sure they were used for some war material. And a certain quota was assigned every day. And she who didn't make the quota-- and most of us didn't-- were badly kicked, beaten, slapped by the [GERMAN], by the SS woman in charge. [SIGHS] The only advantage, if it can be called that, the only advantage of working in the Weberei was that you were in an enclosed environment. And various SS couldn't grab you for various things, or you weren't hit, you weren't beaten by anyone else except the SS supervisor. Well, one nice day, when we went out early in the morning to stand [? seil ?] appell, an SS came, conversed, conferred with the Lager [GERMAN]. And we were told to go back into our blocks. Later on we found out, within a day or so, that the Weberei, which consisted of one big building-- as I remember made of wood, and it had several compartments where we worked-- this place was blown up. It didn't exist. That was the end of it. I am jumping ahead. About 10 years ago, I found out what happened at a wedding in Vail, Colorado. It was winter, and we were in Vail. And the son of friends of ours was marrying a young woman who lived in Colorado, Vail. We were at that wedding. The bride's family was European. And I met several people. And there was a man from Czechoslovakia, who started talking to me. And somehow, survivors have a way of connecting immediately. And I asked him where he was. And he told me, and then he asked me where I was, and I told him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he says, you were in Auschwitz-Birkenau? Yes. Where did you work? Well, I said, I worked at the Brzezinka-- and then in a commando called Brzezinka and then at the Weberei. And at that point, he raises his arms, says, my god, you worked at the Weberei? I said, yes. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could see that he was very touched and very emotional about it. Eventually, he managed to tell me the following story. He too was in Auschwitz, which I knew from before. And he was somehow connected with the underground in Auschwitz, which there was an underground, as we know today. And somehow, that underground learned that at a certain day, the women working at the Weberei, in the evening at sundown, when they have finished their work, they will be marched. Instead of back to Lager B, they will be marched straight to the crematoria. Then, it was decided that before the night was over, they are going to blow up the Weberei.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And therefore, the women couldn't go there next morning, because there was no Weberei anymore. The Weberei was blown up. That's why they didn't take us to the Weberei that morning. Because in the evening, that commando was stated to be exterminated. And this is the man, here in America, 1985 approximately, telling me how he, unknown to me, unknown to him, saved my life. So that was the end of the Weberei commando.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to put it in context, what did you know at the time? One day, it was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: We just knew that it was demolished. Why it was demolished, we had no idea why.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you didn't know the date. Again, it was fall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fall, that much I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this man's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. He was from Czechoslovakia. You know, at the wedding, you meet so many people and new names. Really, I don't remember. But if it's important, I can find out, because I know the family whose daughter was getting married there. And he was a friend of theirs, living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's really incredible how accidents, coincidences, can make the difference between life and death. Did you know what was going on with the resistance in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. The only time we realized that there was a resistance was when one of the crematoria was exploded. And I remember hearing the shots and the running and sirens, the wailing of the sirens. But at that point, there was this rumor that there was an underground. But again, the most important thing was food. Yeah, well, there was the underground, and we were very elated that one of the crematoria was leveled or damaged, at any rate. But there were several more left. And it was that sort of a desire, come on, do it, even if it takes our lives. But do it, do it. I had that feeling earlier in the fall. I keep backtracking. But the flood of memories are really coming now infinitely more than I thought they would.

speaker: interviewer
text: So sometimes, early in the fall, there was the sirens, blaring of sirens, some sort of an air raid. I don't know why the sirens were blaring, although I know they were not blaring for our benefit, for the prisoners' benefit. But they were blaring for the benefit of the SS personnel to go into air raid shelters, which they probably did. All I know is that we all ran out, wherever we were. So I must have-- actually, I was working in the Brzezinka, so it was during the day. Because I was on the night shift, and this was during the day. And we all ran out, looked up, and there were planes flying over Auschwitz-Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since it was an air raid, we never even imagined that they could have been Russian planes or American planes. We kept yelling, 'Tommy, Tommy, Tommy.' The English, we used to call them, 'Tommy.' Give it to them. Drop the bombs. Those bombs would have hit us. But we said, drop the bombs. Drop it, give it to them. The planes flew over, nothing happened. That was the end of the air raid. X number of years later, I am in America, married to my husband who is a lawyer, a graduate of Columbia Law. About 25 years ago, there is a commemorative dinner of the law faculty, et cetera. We are invited. And by the way, I told my husband after we got married that there were those planes which flew over Auschwitz. Because the story was whenever Jewish organizations or people would ask, why didn't the Allies bomb the railroad leading to Auschwitz, the answer always was, it wasn't feasible. It was too complicated, et cetera, et cetera. And the first time I read that in the newspaper, I turned to my husband and I said, that's not true. I saw planes, I remember. And I told him the story I just told you. And my husband, who is a decorated Second World War captain, was in the air corps. And he felt very put upon, because he was defending his army and then his corps. And he said, no, no, no, no, no, you must have seen something else. Perhaps they were Russian planes, because that might have been closer. It couldn't have been. We couldn't bomb, it was technically impossible, the logistics, and so forth. All right, I let it go. Now, I am back, some 20-odd years later at this dinner at Columbia. And my husband is at my right, and a very nice gentleman is my dinner partner at my left. And it is sometimes June, July, summer. I have a short sleeve, and we start talking. And he looks at me, and he sees the number on my arm. And he says, oh, you were in Auschwitz, which stunned me because from the plate card and the name, I knew he was not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, well-- and how could he have known that this means Auschwitz? And I said, well, how do you know? Oh, he says, in the fall of 1944, I was a pilot in a plane flying over Auschwitz. Then, I turned to my husband and I said, now you believe me? They were not English planes. They were not the Tommys.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were American planes. Oh, they flew over Auschwitz and they knew. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS] You were just after--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Weberei. Yes. Then came the revolt of the Sonderkommando, who blew up one of the crematoria. But that was late in the fall. And there were no more transports coming in. But there was something peculiar in the air. You could sense that things weren't going exactly the way the Nazis planned. And work ceased in the Weberei, and they put us in Lager C, which I called 'anus mundi,' the 'world's anus,' which was the worst. This-- we are now in late November, the weather is terrible, cold, raining. The roofs leak. There are puddles in this camp. Nothing is organized. Whoever can steal a potato has what to eat. Whoever doesn't, starves.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you surviving?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is the worst. I was constantly on the garbage piles, looking for food. And I remembered one of the girls with whom I went, who was from my town, Sighet, and knew me from before. We went to dig, to scavenge for whatever we could get. And we were scavenging in this pile of garbage, near the kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she turns to me and she said, hey, Bubba, are you crazy? You are scavenging with a fork? Use your fingers. Where do you think you are, at your mother's table?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't even aware. I must have had organized some fork and I was-- with a group of Greek women, I remember I joined them. And we went because they wanted some women to carry-- there was some sort of a kitchen, where they boiled potatoes like once in three days, and they distributed a potato whoever grabbed it. It was chaos. It was total chaos. With three Greek women-- I was the fourth one-- we volunteered to go to the kitchen and carry the kubel, the barrel of potato or whatever food, to carry to wherever they tell us to carry. We come to the kitchen, and they give us a barrel full of potatoes, boiled potatoes. I very well remember the leader of this group was a tall, dark-haired Greek woman. And we start carrying it. The barrel is on-- what do you call those, long handles. And each girl holds onto a handle. And we start walking towards the direction that we were told to walk. And suddenly, she says, we make a left turn now, and we steal the barrel full of potatoes, which we did. We went behind one of the wooden shacks, stuffed ourselves literally with the potatoes, called other girls, come and get it-- because this is only as much as we could take-- and walked away. Now, the SS woman somehow found out that the leader of this was the Greek girl. And it was chaos by that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was at the point where nobody cared. Do something, what can you do to me? Take me to the crematoria? Take me. So it's going to be over. My misery is going to be over. So this SS woman comes to the Greek girl and says, OK, you stole it. But where is the kubel? Where is the barrel? She says, I don't know where the barrel is. Maybe it's somewhere behind-- there was this sort of, what can you do? What more can you do to me? And maybe if you take me to the crematoria, it's going to be a relief. So she says to her, well, it's over there. So the SS woman says, go and get it. She says--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you translate for those not in the know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. 'I should go and get it? It's not my kubel, it's not my barrel. It's Hitler's barrel. Let him go and get it.' All right? But hunger was terrible. Some of the women began to really fantasize about Lucullan feasts, chicken and partridge and roasts and God knows what. And as a result, they became [GERMAN], meaning totally skeletal. You could see the eyes were blurry and gone. And eventually, they were just taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we are probably the end of December 1944. Again, there is something in the air that the war doesn't go the way expected. And this is why it was this attitude, what can you do to me? Go and sue me. So you'll send me to the crematoria. You'll do me a favor.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened was they began to take people-- women, I'm talking only about women because it was a women's camp. And some of the transports we heard were marched to death. They were the death marches. Other transports were put in trains and then blown up somewhere. Well, nobody knew what was really happening. I didn't have shoes by this time. And I secured some sort of a wooden [GERMAN], they used to call it. And my feet, first of all, it was cutting into the flesh, and they were falling off. It was worse with the shoes on than without. I remembered I had literally dreams of gigantic shoes, padded, soft, night after night. And it was really a misery. I couldn't stand it anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, there came a day when they said that they are looking for workers in some factory somewhere. Again, nobody tells you where, what. What can happen? It can be true, it can be another lie.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I'm going. I cannot take it. The shoes-- I was walking barefoot in mud, in rain. It began to snow. It was December, it was dreadful. And another woman from my hometown said, I'll go, too. Well, we went. They picked about 150 women, end of December. And indeed, clearly, the train was not blown up. We ended up in a labor camp. From an extermination camp, now we are in a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month? You said December.

speaker: interviewee
text: End of December.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: End of December. But of course, looking back, one more month, and we would have been liberated because the Russians were there. But we didn't know. In this labor camp, we worked in a factory. It was in Weisswasser.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. Yes and they-- well, the Nazis didn't do it for our benefit. But they did it for the benefit of the rest of the factory workers, so they shouldn't see that kind of a thing. It's in Bohemia someplace. I've tried to find it on the map. You cannot find it on the map. But this other woman, who I call my 'camp sister,' and I, because we were liberated together-- in fact, we shared a bunk-- she found out where this place was, and that it was a telephone factory, where they had slave laborers. And we were part of it. And we were in close quarters. Again, it was a step up from Lager C, because there was one washroom for about 150 women. And eventually, you could get to a washroom, like once a week, perhaps. And now and then, they did give us a hot meal. But more importantly, right next to us-- I was assigned to test something, which I always suspected that they were parts to an airplane, some little parts to an airplane. And if the testing was not positive, I had to put in a little slip and say, this is where it didn't test and put it on a shelf, where a Frenchman-- now, these are the so-called 'volunteer' French workers who volunteered to go and work in Germany, which as one of them said to me-- [SPEAKING FRENCH] 'We were like check pieces--' chess pieces, I mean, I'm sorry-- 'taken from one place to another.' They didn't volunteer. They were forced. And they were then supposed to fix it. But they didn't fix it, either. So it was this sort of unspoken sabotage going on. And these Frenchmen gave-- we were about six Jewish slave laborers there at a separate table with an [GERMAN] constantly watching over us-- somehow managed every day to slip a piece of bread, a piece of fruit, and if not that, a letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when Roosevelt died-- I'll never forget that, April 19, 1945-- I found that out because one of the Frenchmen sent this note saying, Roosevelt died, but do not give up hope. The war is coming to an end.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was letters from them to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It wasn't to me personally, but I would pick them and translate and reply. It was to all of us. They were very decent, very decent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about your camp sister, your friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: My camp sister worked in another department. I was working in this testing department. She worked in another department, where she had a very difficult job. I think when they asked her what was her profession, I don't know what made her say that she was a metal worker, which wasn't true. Because she graduated a couple of years before me. And if anything, she was not a metal worker. I think eventually, she became a doctor. And being a metal worker, they put her in one of the most difficult spots-- to lift things, to lift machinery, to carry machinery from one place to another, with a couple of other girls. So she had it very, very bad. But we shared whatever we had. And if it can be said, it was sort of upgrading, being upgraded from Lager C from Auschwitz-Birkenau, clearly. But there, too, for instance, most Germans say, we didn't know, we didn't know. Well, they didn't know that Dachau was right next to Munich. And they didn't know, for instance, the villagers in Weisswasser, they didn't know that they had slave laborers. And my answer is now, come on. Every morning at dawn and every evening at sunset, you saw a group of 150 women being herded into a column. And one of the women walking in front with a sign which says-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Attention or careful, Jews are coming.' And there was a bell. And you mean to tell me that those villagers didn't see that, day in and day out? In the morning and in the evening? This is how we went to work and came back from work into the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: But nobody knew. Nobody knew. Nobody heard anything. Nobody saw anything. It hurts, it hurts. What hurts most of all is the denial of truth. And by denying that what happened to us is inflicting the pain almost all over again. Eventually, one nice day, it was a Saturday. And we knew that the foremen were all SS people, although they wore a white robe-- not robe, a white-- what do you call, sort of a white shirt or something over their uniform. They knew they were SS. And there was one particular mean-looking guy in my section that we didn't even dare look at him. Came this particular Saturday, and he passes by. And the six of us were seated at a long table, where we were testing. And I was at one end. He passes by. And as he passes by, he puts something on my work counter. I thought I'm going to faint, because I didn't know what to do. If I'm taking it and I touch it, he's going to find this as a crime, and he'll cut what little hair I had or beat me or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Should I open it? Shouldn't I open it? I didn't know what to do. We have to change tapes.
